perturbation.original_text: With respect to any INFORMATION known by RECIPIENT prior to the time of disclosure by OWNER that RECIPIENT believes to constitute the INFORMATION, or any portion thereof, RECIPIENT shall disclose to OWNER an adequate written description of the INFORMATION within fourteen (14) days of the disclosure by OWNER.
perturbation.changed_text: With respect to any INFORMATION known by RECIPIENT prior to the time of disclosure by OWNER that RECIPIENT believes to constitute the INFORMATION, or any portion thereof, RECIPIENT shall disclose to OWNER an adequate written description of the INFORMATION within five (5) days of the disclosure by OWNER.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the disclosure timeframe to 5 days may be insufficient under certain trade secret laws, particularly if reverse engineering or complex analysis is required to identify the information. This creates a potentially unreasonable burden on the recipient.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law requires a reasonable opportunity to determine if information constitutes a trade secret. A five-day window may be insufficient for complex information, potentially violating CUTSA.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.6. An action for misappropriation must be brought within three years after the misappropriation is discovered or by the exercise of reasonable diligence should have been discovered. For the purposes of this section, a continuing misappropriation constitutes a single claim. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The original text allows 14 days for disclosure, while the changed text reduces this to 5 days. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both suggest that 5 days may be insufficient under trade secret laws, particularly when reverse engineering or complex analysis is required. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, where time-sensitive obligations in a contract do not align with legal requirements. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 focuses on the statute of limitations for misappropriation actions (3 years), it doesn't directly address the reasonableness of a 5-day disclosure window. However, the Law Explanation argues that the reduced timeframe may violate CUTSA by not providing a reasonable opportunity to determine if information constitutes a trade secret. Therefore, reducing the timeframe to 5 days creates a contradiction as it potentially violates trade secret laws.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall remain in force in spite of disclosure of the INFORMATION by OWNER in the form of patent applications, copyright a pplications, or other disclosures by OWNER.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall terminate immediately upon disclosure of the INFORMATION by OWNER in the form of patent applications, copyright a pplications, or other disclosures by OWNER.
perturbation.explanation: Premature termination upon public disclosure contradicts standard intellectual property protection timelines. Disclosure typically triggers ongoing protection, not immediate termination of confidentiality obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: Patent law grants inventors the right to exclude others from making, using, or selling the invention for a term of 20 years from the date of application filing, not immediate termination of any agreement after application.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 35 U.S. Code § 154 - Contents and term of patent; provisional rights | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 35 U.S. Code § 154 - Contents and term of patent; provisional rights U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) In General.— (1) Contents.— Every patent shall contain a short title of the invention and a grant to the patentee, his heirs or assigns, of the right to exclude others from making, using, offering for sale, or selling the invention throughout the United States or importing the invention into the United States, and, if the invention is a process, of the right to exclude others from using, offering for sale or selling throughout the United States, or importing into the United States, products made by that process, referring to the specification for the particulars thereof. (2) Term.— Subject to the payment of fees under this title, such grant shall be for a term beginning on the date on which the patent issues and ending 20 years from the date on which the application for the patent was filed in the United States or, if the application contains a specific reference to an earlier filed application or applications under section 120, 121, 365(c), or 386(c), from the date on which the earliest such application was filed. (3) Priority.— Priority under section 119 , 365(a) , 365(b) , 386(a) , or 386(b) shall not be taken into account in determining the term of a patent. (4) Specification and drawing.— A copy of the specification and drawing shall be annexed to the patent and be a part of such patent. (b) Adjustment of Patent Term.— (1) Patent term guarantees.— (A) Guarantee of prompt patent and trademark office responses.— Subject to the limitations under paragraph (2), if the issue of an original patent is delayed due to the failure of the Patent and Trademark Office to— (i) provide at least one of the notifications under section 132 or a notice of allowance under section 151 n

Contents and term of patent; provisional rights (a) In General.- (1) Contents .-Every patent shall contain a short title of the invention and a grant to the patentee, his heirs or assigns, of the right to exclude others from making, using, offering for sale, or selling the invention throughout the United States or importing the invention into the Unit
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the purpose of patent law, which is to grant inventors a period of exclusivity, as stated in 35 U.S. Code § 154. The original text maintains the agreement despite disclosure, aligning with the intent to protect intellectual property during the patent application process and beyond, if a patent is granted. The changed text terminates the agreement upon disclosure, which negates any potential protection that patent law intends to provide. The scraped snippets both highlight the inventor's right to exclude others for a set period, which is directly undermined by terminating the agreement upon disclosure. The modification removes a core legal protection, thus a legal contradiction is present.

perturbation.original_text: RECIPIENT agrees that should RECIPIENT breach a ny of the promises contained in this Agreement that OWNER would suffer irreparable harm and OWNER w ould be without adequate remedy at law and that OWNER may obtain injunctive relief, including specific performance of the Agreement, as well as monetary award for damages suffered by OWNER for RECIPIENT’S breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: RECIPIENT agrees that should RECIPIENT breach a ny of the promises contained in this Agreement, OWNER's sole and exclusive remedy shall be liquidated damages of $100, regardless of actual harm or loss suffered by OWNER.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting remedies to a fixed $100 may be unconscionable and unenforceable, especially in cases of significant trade secret misappropriation where damages could be substantially higher. This creates an imbalance that contradicts standard principles of contract law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law states that a liquidated damages clause is void unless it is reasonable under the circumstances. A fixed amount of $100 for any breach, including significant trade secret theft, is likely unreasonable and unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1671 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4.5. LIQUIDATED DAMAGES [1671 - 1681] ( Heading of Title 4.5 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 198. ) CHAPTER 1. General Provisions [1671- 1671.] ( Heading of Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 198. ) 1671. (a) This section does not apply in any case where another statute expressly applicable to the contract prescribes the rules or standard for determining the validity of a provision in the contract liquidating the damages for the breach of the contract. (b) Except as provided in subdivision (c), a provision in a contract liquidating the damages for the breach of the contract is valid unless the party seeking to invalidate the provision establishes that the provision was unreasonable under the circumstances existing at the time the contract was made. (c) The validity of a liquidated damages provision shall be determined under subdivision (d) and not under subdivision (b) where the liquidated damages are sought to be recovered from either: (1) A party to a contract for the retail purchase, or rental, by such party of personal property or services, primarily for the party’s personal, famil
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts California Civil Code Section 1671, which addresses liquidated damages. The original text allows for injunctive relief and monetary damages. The changed text limits the OWNER's remedy to a fixed $100, regardless of the actual harm. Section 1671(b) states that a liquidated damages provision is valid unless proven unreasonable. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both argue that a fixed $100 is likely unreasonable, especially in cases like trade secret theft where damages could be significantly higher. This limitation directly opposes the potential for reasonable compensation as suggested by the code, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, construed, and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of California in the Central District of California. Any dispute involving the terms or conditions of this Agreement shall be brought in the Central District of California or a California State court of competent subject matter jurisdiction therein. Each of the parties he reby submits to the personal jurisdiction of said court.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, construed, and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to its conflict of laws principles. Any dispute involving the terms or conditions of this Agreement shall be resolved exclusively through binding arbitration in North Korea, under North Korean law. Each of the parties hereby submits to the jurisdiction of said arbitration process.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware and requiring arbitration in North Korea with North Korean law is highly problematic. While parties can generally agree to arbitration, choosing a jurisdiction with a legal system that lacks transparency, fairness, and due process is likely unenforceable under California law and public policy.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law allows for vacating an arbitration award if the arbitrator exceeded their powers or if the award violates public policy. Requiring arbitration under North Korean law would likely be seen as violating California public policy and could render the arbitration clause unenforceable.

OF SPECIAL PROCEEDINGS OF A CIVIL NATURE [1063 - 1822.60] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 9. ARBITRATION [1280 - 1294.4] ( Title 9 repealed and added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) CHAPTER 4. Enforcement of the Award [1285 - 1288.8] ( Chapter 4 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) ARTICLE 1. Confirmation, Correction or Vacation of the Award [1285 - 1287.6] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) 1286.2. (a) Subject to Section 1286.4, the court shall vacate the award if the court determines any of the following: (1) The award was procured by corruption, fraud or other undue means. (2) There was corruption in any of the arbitrators. (3) The rights of the party were substantially prejudiced by misconduct of a neutral arbitrator. (4) The arbitrators exceeded their powers and the award cannot be corrected without affecting the merits of the decision upon the controversy submitted. (5) The rights of the party were substantially prejudiced by the refusal of the arbitrators to postpone the hearing upon sufficient cause being shown therefor or by the refusal of the arbitrators to hear evidence material to the controversy or by other conduct of the arbitrators contrary to the provisions of this title. (6) An arbitrator making the award either
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California law. The original text specified California law and courts for dispute resolution. The changed text mandates arbitration in North Korea under North Korean law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CCP 1286.2(a), states that a court 'shall vacate the award if the court determines' that '[t]he arbitrators exceeded their powers'. Requiring arbitration under North Korean law could be viewed as the arbitrators exceeding their powers under California law or the award violating California public policy, supporting a finding that the modification creates an unenforceable agreement under California law and thus contradicting the law.
